title: An Open Economy DSGE Model with Search-and-Matching Frictions: The Case of Hungary
abstract: This article builds and estimates a medium scale, small open economy DSGE model augmented with search-and-matching frictions in the labor market, and different wage setting behavior in new and existing jobs. The model is estimated using Hungarian data between 2001–2008. We find that: (i) the inclusion of matching frictions significantly improves the model’s empirical fit; (ii) the extent of new hires wage rigidity is quantitatively important for key macro variables; (iii) labor market shocks do not play an important role in inflation dynamics, but the structure of the labor market influences the monetary transmission mechanism.
summary_en: How well do Hungarian business-cycle dynamics fit a medium-scale DSGE model augmented with labour-market search and matching? This paper builds and estimates such a model with different wage-setting for new and existing jobs. Using Hungarian data 2001–2008, three findings: (i) matching frictions significantly improve the model's fit; (ii) rigidity in new-hire wages quantitatively matters for macro variables; (iii) labour-market shocks don't drive inflation but labour-market structure shapes the monetary-transmission mechanism. A key reference for Hungarian DSGE modelling.
data_used: Hungarian macroeconomic and labour-market data 2001–2008. Medium-scale open-economy DSGE model with search-and-matching frictions.
policy_relevance: Foundational for MNB monetary-policy modelling and analysis. The paper shows why labour-market structure matters for Hungarian monetary-transmission — supporting careful modelling of new-hire wage dynamics and matching frictions in macro forecasts. Caveat: data end pre-2008 crisis; post-crisis Hungarian labour market dynamics differ.
